Press enter or click to view image in full size

The bug that made my trades happen before the market opened
As a senior specializing in Computer Science, I started day trading and found myself constantly against my own rules without being aware of it. Because of this problem, I developed TradeGuard, a self-hosted trading log and open-source project that allows you to download trades directly from your broker’s CSV export while taking into consideration all the rules you established for yourself. Whether it is the maximum allowable risk for every trade, requiring a stop loss or avoiding revenge trading, the program will inform you about the rule you broke and its circumstances.
The backend is built using Python and FastAPI and uses SQLite, while the front-end is powered by Next.js with the importer being done e xactly as you would expect it to work.
At that point I extracted my exact order history from Webull and directed it to my own application. My application crashed in three different instances within a span of three days. This blog post is dedicated to explaining those three bugs and their role in imparting valuable lessons about processing data.
Bug 1: my broker’s file didn’t match my broker’s file
The importer was built against Webull’s fills export, which has columns like this:
My real export had these instead:
The broker is the same, the account type is the same, but the schema is quite different as there is one column called “undefined” which is an indication of an issue in the frontend.
Furthermore, this file isn’t a list of fills like it should be, but a list of orders, including pending and canceled orders, which means that a partially filled order can have a Qty of 80 and a Filled Qty of 50. If you import the column incorrectly, every time you have a partial fill, you will have a problem in your position calculations.
The solution was to implement automatic detection for both layouts: analyze the heading, select the necessary mapping, thus importing records that were successful in being filled only, while tallying the ignored columns for the information of the user. What I appreciate now is the fact that the importer was made to make mistakes distinctly: many columns that were supposed to be present and only those that were actually indicated were listed in the message regarding the error. Thus, every problem turned into a ten-minute task instead of several hours spent processing data.frames.
Bug 3: the trade that happened before the market opened
Now that the parsing issues have been fixed, the imports have finally succeeded. The trade data is available and the PnL calculations are complete, so everything seems to have been accomplished. However, I went ahead and examined the trades table.
One of the transactions in my portfolio shows that the market order was executed at 6:47 A.M. in the Eastern Time zone, even though extended market hours have been disabled. The market starts functioning at 9:30 A.M., and a regular market order cannot be executed before that time. The information was inaccurate, but I imported it confidently.
The clue was in the one line only. Just one order in my export had ‘Include Extended Hours’ checked = sell with a timestamp of 14:36. Assuming the timestamps were Eastern, with 14:36 in the middle of the trading day, an extended hours order becomes meaningless. But living in Arizona poses a challenge. If the timestamps were in my laptop’s local time, then 14:36 is now 17:36 Eastern, which is after hours, and perfectly in line with an extended hours order. The apparently impossible fill at 6:47 has now become 9:47, which is just after the market opens.
All lines clicked in place immediately. Webull gives you timestamps in your device’s local timezone and does not declare what its time zone is.
The solution depended on the same thing as bug 2: a dropdown with timezones available on the import screen and everything saved in UTC, but rendered in Eastern timezone. The most frightening moment happened before the solution. While all timestamps were off by three hours, my very own rules engine raised a flag about an entry-window violation of a trade that did not violate anything. The logic used for the audit was correct mathematically, but incorrect factually due to the shifting of the data used for it. Bad data leads to good stupidity.
Bug 4: the paper account speaks a third dialect
Later I exported from my Webull paper trading account, mostly to test the importer, and got a third incompatible format:
Capitalization variations affect the mapping. Time of placing is the moment of click on the order. Time of closing is actual execution. No filled quantity meaning that partial execution in this format is an unsizeable order where the importer simply allows no records instead of making any guesses. Also, the symbols come exchange-prefixed, for example, NYSE:GSUN so that we can join paper trades and real trades together under one ticker.
Just one broker. Three formats and this is my entire justification for building the importer the defensive way.
What I actually learned
Test suite of raw user information is something that cannot be prepared in advance. Though my synthetic test data seemed sufficient, it didn’t include any of what I have dealt with because I could only visualize those formats that I have already encountered before.
Public failures are more helpful than silent assumptions. Error messages comparing what we got and what we were supposed to get did their job of reducing the costs of this whole process tremendously.
Assumptions wish to become parameters. Time zone was always an assumption. The problem was that it was hidden.
Idempotency makes it possible for a user to avoid debugging in itself. Filling the deduplication field with a content hash eliminates the need to do anything stupid with the import batch after fixing a bug, therefore re-importing is a safe thing to do. I did it more than once because of my own mistakes.
And ensure you verify the calculations physically at least once. After the initial error-free import, I used a calculator to recalculate my profit and loss statement from the original CSV file using FIFO in a stepwise manner. I found the results coinciding with those of the application and because of that manual check, I trust this program completely.
As for the story of how it was built: I designed and provided technical specifications for TradeGuard, the majority of the coding was done by Claude Code while I was in charge of the general architecture, reviewing the output, and testing it with real data. I caught all the bugs mentioned in this post because I ran my own broker data through my application.
You can host TradeGuard with no trouble and run it via Docker. You can access the repository at https://github.com/gunna17671672/tradeguard in case you wish to find out more about the program.
In case you are curious about whether discipline succeded: the very first time I uploaded genuine data from my trades, I got 0.0% adherence. The program works. And this is the issue.
